## Creative AI tools are useful when the goal is exploration, not just answers

Generative AI is not only about text. In `BIK 2551`, participants also
encounter tools that create images, presentations, video, or visual drafts. The
point is not to master every platform, but to understand which kinds of tasks
these tools are actually good for in day-to-day work.

## Three main categories of creative tools

Most creative AI tools fall into one of three categories:

- **Image generators** create illustrations, simple visualizations, or idea
  sketches from text descriptions.
- **Presentation tools** suggest structure, slides, and visual layouts from a
  topic or a text draft.
- **Multimodal workspaces** combine text, images, audio, and diagrams in one
  process.

The most important distinction is not between brand names. It is between what
you want to achieve: idea development, communication, or finished production.

## When these tools actually create value

Creative tools are especially useful when you need:

- a quick first version of a visual expression,
- several alternative ways to present the same message,
- support for making complex material easier to communicate.

They work less well when the demands for domain precision, visual identity, or
legal control are high. An AI-generated image may be good enough for ideation,
but not necessarily for external publication without quality control.

## Use creative tools as collaborators

The best workflow is often iterative:

1. Start with a clear task and a concrete audience.
2. Ask the tool to generate several options, not one "finished" solution.
3. Choose the best draft and improve it manually.
4. Quality-check language, facts, graphics, and rights before use.

That makes the tools useful as creative sparring partners, not autonomous
producers.

## Licensing, copyright, and reputational risk

Creative AI tools raise their own questions about copyright, licensing, and
internal brand use. Before using generated material at work, clarify:

- whether the tool can be used in a commercial or institutional context,
- whether the organization has guidelines for generated images and video,
- whether the content could be mistaken for documentation or fact when it is
  really illustration.

This matters especially when AI is used in presentations, customer dialogue, or
teaching.

::: {.teacher-only}
Show two quick contrasts: one image-generation tool and one presentation tool.
Afterward, discuss what actually saved time and what still required human
editing to become good enough.
:::

## Where this topic fits in `BIK 2551`

This module expands participants' understanding of what generative AI can be
used for beyond text. It fits well between basic prompting and project work,
because many participants will use AI to communicate ideas, not just to analyze
them.

## Practical points to keep

- Choose tools based on the task, not the hype.
- Use creative tools for ideation and variation, not blind final production.
- Always quality-check content that will be shared with others.
- Clarify licensing and internal policy before using generated material
  externally.
